metadata.title: Claude, Typed.
metadata.description: Skill teardown of the terminal-screencast — the CLI video spine: problem → build → run → check → change, with the Revision Law and Actual-Code Law.

beats.narration_text: Merhaba — this is Liam, in for Bear. The terminal-screencast skill builds CLI videos: a simulated Claude session alternating with animated output. Show the prompt. Show the actual code. Show the output moving. At least one revision. This episode is that spine explaining its own requirements.

output: Template: Claude session + animated output, Teardown register
Law: show prompt → show code → show output; always revise
Pipeline: PROBLEM → ASK → CODE → OUTPUT → CHANGE → SUMMARY

beats.narration_text: The skill shares a skeleton with the claude-explainer. Both have identical Claude-branded bookends — the cold-open composer, the your-turn handoff, the title outro. The middle is what differs: a CLI video shows a build loop, not a teaching structure. The reference folder holds the beat spine template and the example beat sheets. The spine is the discipline.

props.title: Spine is the discipline.
props.calloutText: claude-cli and claude-explainer share identical bookends — only the middle differs
props.calloutSub: PROBLEM → ASK → CODE → OUTPUT → CHANGE is the body.
props.sparkLine: The build loop is the video.

beats.narration_text: The required spine: cold open, then PROBLEM — the stakes before any prompt. Then the first cycle: CLI prompt, show and discuss the code, show the animated output. Then the revision cycle: check the output, issue the changed prompt, show the new code, show the better output. Then SUMMARY and HANDOFF before the outro. All mandatory in the sixteen-nine cut.

props.title: Prompt. Code. Output. Revise.

phases.label: PROBLEM
phases.desc: stakes before any prompt — earn the build

props.footerNote: 16:9 requires at least one revision cycle. 9:16 Shorts: one cycle only, ≤3:00.

beats.narration_text: To demo the plan phase — free, no audio spend — ask it: build a UV catastrophe simulation. The skill identifies the beat spine, drafts the generation prompt, and plans the revision. That output is what the gate reviews before any audio or code generates.

props.segment: Run it on a build concept
output: * concept: UV catastrophe — Rayleigh-Jeans vs Planck
* spine: PROBLEM → ASK → CODE → OUTPUT → CHANGE → SUMMARY
* revision: temperature parameter change → Planck fit
* GATE P required before audio spend

beats.narration_text: The plan output shows the beat spine. Beat two is PROBLEM: why the Rayleigh-Jeans law predicts infinite energy at short wavelengths — the ultraviolet catastrophe, the crisis that broke classical physics. Beat three is ASK: the generation prompt for the Manim scene — the actual command that plausibly produces the code in beat four. Beat five is OUTPUT: the curve drawing. Beat six is CHANGE: add the Planck distribution.

props.title: beat spine plan — terminal-screencast output
props.code:
B00  ClaudeComposerAsk  INTRO — "Merhaba, Liam"
     ./art terminal-screencast 'UV catastrophe'

B01  ClaudeComposerAsk  PROBLEM
     "Classical physics predicts the universe glows infinitely
      bright in ultraviolet. It doesn't."

B02  ClaudeComposerAsk  ASK — generation prompt
     claude "Manim: plot Rayleigh-Jeans B(λ,T) and Planck B(λ,T)
     for T=5000K, λ=100nm–2000nm. Divergence visible."

B03  ClaudeCodeBeat  CODE — actual generated scene
     # key line: B_RJ = 2*c*k*T / lam**4   ← the divergence

B04  OUTPUT  (Manim: curve drawing)
B05  ClaudeComposerAsk  CHANGE — add Planck to same axes
props.sparkLine: Prompt → code → output is one receipt.

beats.narration_text: The problem beat is not optional. A CLI video that opens directly on a prompt skips the stakes. The viewer has no reason to care whether the simulation runs. The problem beat establishes what is broken, why it matters, and what the build is trying to show. Only then does the first prompt earn its place. The spine enforces this — the problem beat is the first mandatory checkpoint before any code appears.

props.heading: Problem first. Prompt second.
props.body: The required spine opens with PROBLEM — what is broken, why it matters, the stakes — before the first generation prompt appears. A CLI video that skips PROBLEM opens on a prompt the viewer has no reason to follow. The PROBLEM beat earns the build. After that the loop begins: CLI prompt shown and discussed, code shown and discussed, output moving, revision. The spine is not optional because each beat in it does a specific job the others cannot.
props.quote: show prompt → show code → show output

beats.narration_text: The revision law is the skill's structural requirement: every sixteen-nine CLI video contains at least one revision cycle. Show the output. Check it against what was expected. Issue a precise follow-up. Show the improved output. A CLI video without a check-and-change is a demo, not a lesson. The learner needs to see that the first output is not the answer — the loop that closes is the skill.

props.heading: One prompt is not enough.
props.body: The Revision Law: a 16:9 CLI video always contains at least one revision cycle — a second CLI→CODE→better OUTPUT sequence. The viewer needs to see the check: look at the output, identify what is missing or wrong, issue the precise change command, watch the difference appear. Without the revision, the video teaches 'type a prompt and get an output.' With the revision, it teaches 'check what you got and close the loop.'
props.quote: a CLI video without a check-and-change is incomplete.

beats.narration_text: The design tell is the actual-code law. The CODE beat shows the real source of the artifact — trimmed to the lines that teach, never pseudocode, never prose restyled as code. The prompt shown must plausibly generate that code. The code must plausibly produce the output. Prompt, code, output is one receipt. If any link is faked, the receipt is a prop. The viewer learns nothing about how to actually build with Claude.

props.heading: Prompt → code → output is one receipt.
props.body: The Actual-Code Law: the CODE beat shows the real source of the artifact — the sim's own Python, the generated scenes.py, the script that actually ran — trimmed to the lines that teach, never pseudocode. The reel reconstructs how the artifact was built with Claude. If the prompt shown could not plausibly generate the code shown, the receipt is a prop. The viewer learns what a CLI video looks like, not how to build one.
props.quote: The reel is a reconstruction of how the artifact was built with Claude: the ASK prompt must plausibly generate the code shown, and the code shown must plausibly produce the OUTPUT beat.

beats.narration_text: What the terminal-screencast skill actually does: it enforces a build loop — problem before prompts, actual code not pseudocode, at least one revision in every sixteen-nine cut. The three laws together mean the viewer watches someone actually build something with Claude, not a choreographed simulation of it. The loop teaches. The laws protect it.

artifactLines: Spine law: PROBLEM earns the build before the first prompt appears.
Revision law: at least one check→change cycle in every 16:9 cut.
Actual-code law: real source trimmed to the teaching lines — no pseudocode.
Receipt law: prompt → code → output is one plausible chain.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Use the terminal-screencast skill on any concept you can build with Claude Code. Before you open the terminal, ask: what is broken about the world without this artifact, and what two things would you check to know the first version worked? Name those before you write the first prompt. Then run: ./art terminal-screencast, and your concept in quotes.

output: Use the terminal-screencast skill on any buildable concept.
Before you open the terminal, ask: what is broken about the world
without this artifact, and what two things would prove it works?
Name those before you write the first prompt.
